grammar_topics.topic: Inversions with negative adverbial words and phrases
grammar_topics.explanation_en: Inversion happens when we put a negative adverb or adverbial phrase at the beginning of a sentence for emphasis. The subject and the auxiliary verb (do, does, did, have, can, etc.) change places.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Đảo ngữ xảy ra khi chúng ta đặt một trạng từ hoặc cụm trạng từ mang nghĩa phủ định ở đầu câu để nhấn mạnh. Chủ ngữ và trợ động từ (do, does, did, have, can, v.v.) sẽ đổi chỗ cho nhau.
rules: No sooner + had + S + V3/ed + than...
Only after/Only when/Not until + clause/phrase + auxiliary + S + V...
Under no circumstances / On no account + auxiliary + S + V...

examples.sentence: Hardly had the new law been introduced when the mistake was realised.
examples.translation: Ngay khi luật mới vừa được ban hành thì người ta đã nhận ra sai lầm.

examples.sentence: Only after I had checked that the burglars had left did I call the police.
examples.translation: Chỉ sau khi tôi đã kiểm tra chắc chắn bọn trộm đã đi rồi thì tôi mới gọi cảnh sát.

examples.sentence: Under no circumstances will we accept an increase in working hours.
examples.translation: Trong bất kỳ hoàn cảnh nào, chúng tôi cũng sẽ không chấp nhận việc tăng giờ làm.

watch_out: With 'not until' and 'only (when/after)', the inversion happens in the main clause, not the subordinate clause.
Ensure the correct correlatives are used: Hardly... when, No sooner... than.

grammar_topics.explanation_en: Inversion also occurs after 'here' and 'there', in short answers (so/neither/nor), after 'as/than' in formal contexts, and in conditional sentences without 'if'.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Đảo ngữ cũng xảy ra sau 'here' và 'there', trong các câu trả lời ngắn (so/neither/nor), sau 'as/than' trong ngữ cảnh trang trọng, và trong các câu điều kiện bỏ 'if'.
rules: Here/There + V + S (if S is a noun)

examples.sentence: So rare is burglary here that many people don't bother to lock their doors.
examples.translation: Vụ trộm ở đây hiếm đến mức nhiều người chẳng buồn khóa cửa.

examples.sentence: Had I known about the crime problem, I would never have moved here.
examples.translation: Nếu tôi biết về vấn đề tội phạm, tôi đã không bao giờ chuyển đến đây.

watch_out: If the subject is a pronoun with 'here/there', we don't invert (e.g., Here it comes, NOT Here comes it).

grammar_topics.explanation_en: Cleft sentences are used to focus on a specific part of the sentence. They often start with 'It is/was...', 'What...', or 'All...'.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Câu chẻ được sử dụng để tập trung vào một phần cụ thể của câu. Chúng thường bắt đầu bằng 'It is/was...', 'What...', hoặc 'All...'.
rules: It is/was + [focused part] + who/that + [rest of sentence]
The reason (why) / The person (who) / The place (where) ... is/was ...

examples.sentence: It was Carol who called the police.
examples.translation: Chính là Carol đã gọi cảnh sát.

examples.sentence: What annoys me is the boss's attitude.
examples.translation: Điều làm tôi bực mình chính là thái độ của ông chủ.

examples.sentence: All that Keith wanted was to get his money back.
examples.translation: Tất cả những gì Keith muốn là lấy lại tiền của mình.

watch_out: In 'It is/was...' sentences, if the subject is a pronoun, 'It was I who...' is formal, while 'It was me that...' is informal.

grammar_topics.explanation_en: These words are used to express degree or result.
grammar_topics.explanation_vi: Những từ này được sử dụng để diễn tả mức độ hoặc kết quả.

examples.sentence: It all happened so quickly that I didn't have time to see the man's face.
examples.translation: Mọi việc xảy ra nhanh đến mức tôi không kịp nhìn mặt người đàn ông đó.

examples.sentence: There is such a lot of crime around here that I'm thinking of moving.
examples.translation: Có quá nhiều tội phạm ở quanh đây đến mức tôi đang nghĩ đến việc chuyển đi.

examples.sentence: The police weren't quick enough to catch the burglar.
examples.translation: Cảnh sát đã không đủ nhanh để bắt được tên trộm.

watch_out: Too is used for negative effects (excessive), while very/extremely are just for high degree.
Don't add an extra object at the end of 'too... to' sentences (e.g., This problem is too difficult to solve, NOT solve it).

exercises.label: A
exercises.instruction: If a phrase in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, rewrite it correctly.

items.sentence: *Hardly I had* sat down when the doorbell rang.
items.explanation: When 'Hardly' starts a sentence, we must invert the subject and auxiliary verb.

items.sentence: **Scarcely had Julian** finished writing when the teacher told the students to put their pens down.
items.explanation: Correct inversion after 'Scarcely' at the beginning of the sentence.

items.sentence: **We had barely** set off on our journey when the kids started asking when we would get there.
items.explanation: Correct. Inversion is not required when the negative adverb is in its mid-position.

items.sentence: *No sooner the government changes* the tax laws than businesses find a way around them.
items.answer: No sooner does the government change
items.explanation: Inversion is required after 'No sooner' at the start of the sentence.

items.sentence: *Scarcely they had* finished painting the house when it started to rain.
items.explanation: Inversion is required after 'Scarcely' at the start of the sentence.

items.sentence: *Hardly the new computer system had been* installed when it started to go wrong.
items.answer: Hardly had the new computer system been
items.explanation: Inversion is required after 'Hardly' at the start of the sentence.

items.sentence: It's true that *no sooner we had* started eating than we realised we had forgotten the potatoes.
items.explanation: Inversion is required in the clause starting with 'no sooner'.

items.sentence: Barely had the politician started to speak when *began the crowd to boo*.
items.explanation: No inversion is needed in the 'when' clause of 'Barely... when'.

items.sentence: *Hardly the new park was* finished when vandals destroyed the flowerbeds.
items.explanation: Inversion is required after 'Hardly' at the start of the sentence.

items.sentence: *Scarcely Keith had* arrived in Argentina when he was arrested.
items.explanation: Inversion is required after 'Scarcely' at the start of the sentence.

exercises.label: B
exercises.instruction: Complete using the words and phrases in the box.

items.sentence: ___ were the security guards to blame for what happened.
items.explanation: 'In no way' means 'not at all', fitting the context of blaming the guards.

items.sentence: ___ once did the old woman thank me for helping her.
items.explanation: 'Not once' is a common phrase meaning 'never' and triggers inversion.

items.sentence: ___ seeing the doctor was Theresa allowed to leave hospital.
items.explanation: 'Only after' indicates a sequence of events and triggers inversion in the main clause.

items.sentence: ___ will passengers be allowed to carry more than 6 kg of hand luggage.
items.explanation: This is a formal rule, meaning 'absolutely not allowed'.

items.sentence: ___ were you late, but you also forgot to bring the correct documents.
items.explanation: The presence of 'but... also' indicates the structure 'Not only... but also'.

items.sentence: ___ during the meeting did anyone mention that the managing director was planning to leave.
items.explanation: 'At no point' means 'never' during a specific period of time.

items.sentence: ___ did the passengers know that the driver was really an undercover police officer.
items.explanation: 'Little did someone know' is a common idiom meaning they had no idea.

items.sentence: ___ do you meet anyone as charming as Mr Beeching.
items.explanation: 'Rarely' means 'not often' and fits with the positive description of being charming.

items.sentence: ___ should unaccompanied children be allowed to enter the area.
items.explanation: Similar to 'under no circumstances', it expresses a strict prohibition.

items.sentence: ___ before has the company found itself facing such stiff competition.
items.explanation: 'Never before' is a standard emphatic expression of past experience.

exercises.label: C
exercises.instruction: Complete using the words in bold with the verb in the correct form.

items.sentence: Not until the area is made safe ___ . (construction / can continue)
items.explanation: After 'Not until...', the main clause is inverted (auxiliary + subject + verb).

items.sentence: In no way ___ how strongly we felt about the situation. (the announcement / affect)
items.explanation: Inversion after 'In no way' requires 'did' for the past tense.

items.sentence: Seldom ___ a vintage car in quite such good condition these days. (you / see)
items.explanation: Inversion after 'Seldom' in the present simple requires 'do'.

items.sentence: Not only ___ my birthday, but he also forgot our wedding anniversary. (Jerry / forget)
items.explanation: Inversion after 'Not only' requires 'did' for the past event.

items.sentence: At no time ___ my opinion on the subject. (I / ask)
items.explanation: Passive voice inversion: 'was' (auxiliary) + 'I' (subject) + 'asked' (past participle).

items.sentence: Only when ___ the ticket again did I realise that I had won first prize! (I / checked)
items.explanation: No inversion inside the 'Only when' clause; inversion follows in the main clause ('did I realise').

items.sentence: Little ___ that I had a big surprise planned for her. (Caroline / realise)
items.explanation: Standard inversion with 'Little' to show lack of awareness.

items.sentence: Under no circumstances ___ a contract you haven't read thoroughly. (you / should sign)
items.explanation: Modal 'should' is inverted with the subject 'you' after 'Under no circumstances'.

items.sentence: At no stage ___ likely that Wilkinson would win the championship. (it / appear)
items.explanation: Inversion after 'At no stage' in the past simple.

items.sentence: Only if the weather is clear ___ to launch. (the space shuttle / will allow)
items.answer: will the space shuttle be allowed
items.explanation: The main clause is inverted and requires passive voice ('be allowed').

exercises.label: D
exercises.instruction: Complete using a verb from the box in the correct form. Try to use each verb at least once. (appear, be, come, go, lie, sit, stand)

items.sentence: 'There ___ our new neighbour now,' said Joe.
items.explanation: Present simple 'comes' following 'There' to indicate someone's arrival.

items.sentence: I looked up and coming along the path ___ a short, fat man.
items.explanation: Inversion with a participle phrase. 'Was' is the verb connecting the subject 'a short, fat man'.

items.sentence: On his head ___ a bright red baseball cap.
items.explanation: Inversion with a prepositional phrase, using 'sat' to describe the position of the cap.

items.sentence: From an upstairs window ___ the sound of loud rock music.
items.explanation: Inversion with a prepositional phrase to describe the source of the sound.

items.sentence: In his hand ___ a letter from the council.
items.explanation: Inversion with a prepositional phrase describing what was in Joe's hand.

items.sentence: There ___ a little old woman, looking up at us, smiling.
items.explanation: Inversion after 'There' to describe the woman's position.

items.sentence: On the sofa behind her ___ a fat old cat.
items.explanation: Inversion with a prepositional phrase to describe where the cat was.

items.sentence: Suddenly, in the doorway ___ the same fat man I had seen earlier.
items.explanation: Inversion after a prepositional phrase to describe someone's sudden appearance.

exercises.label: E
exercises.instruction: Circle the correct phrase. If both are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Dave doesn't really trust Ian, and I have to say that ___ .
items.explanation: After 'neither', we use inversion (neither + auxiliary + subject).

items.sentence: ___ that the public are not allowed to get close to it.
options: Such a valuable painting it is
items.explanation: Inversion follows 'Such + adj + noun' for emphasis.

items.sentence: ___ watch TV while we ate, we moved the television onto the balcony.
items.explanation: 'So' here means 'in order that', not an emphatic inversion, so no inversion is used.

items.sentence: My dad's never been abroad, and ___ .
items.explanation: After 'nor', we use inversion.

items.sentence: ___ in the wedding that there were over a dozen photographers.
items.explanation: Inversion follows 'Such + noun' for emphasis.

items.sentence: It's been a hard season for the team, ___ .
items.answer: as last season was / was last season
items.explanation: Both forms are correct after 'as' in formal comparison, though the inverted one is more formal.

items.sentence: Russia is a country with vast natural resources, and ___ .
items.explanation: After 'so' in a short addition meaning 'also', we use inversion.

items.sentence: We in this country spend more per person on fuel than ___ .
items.answer: the French do / do the French
items.explanation: Both are correct after 'than' in formal comparison.

items.sentence: The Minister was forced to resign, ___ on her.
items.explanation: The structure 'such was the pressure' is used for emphasis.

items.sentence: ___ that I was almost certain he was lying.
items.explanation: Inversion follows 'So + adjective' at the start of a sentence for emphasis.

exercises.label: F
exercises.instruction: Rewrite the sentences, starting with the words given.

items.sentence: The window was broken by a cricket ball. -> It ___
items.answer: It was a cricket ball that/which broke the window.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence to focus on the object 'a cricket ball'.

items.sentence: I will never forget the day I heard I'd got into my chosen university. -> The day on ___
items.answer: The day on which I heard I'd got into my chosen university is a day I will never forget.
items.explanation: Using a relative clause starting with 'on which'.

items.sentence: Glynn became a social worker to help people less fortunate than himself. -> The reason ___
items.answer: The reason why Glynn became a social worker was to help people less fortunate than himself.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence focusing on the reason.

items.sentence: Getting through the summer without getting injured was the only thing the athlete wanted. -> All ___
items.answer: All the athlete wanted was to get through the summer without getting injured.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence starting with 'All'.

items.sentence: The driver didn't see the motorcyclist. -> What happened ___
items.answer: What happened was that the driver didn't see the motorcyclist.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence using 'What happened'.

items.sentence: Christopher Columbus discovered America. -> The person ___
items.answer: The person who discovered America was Christopher Columbus.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence focusing on the person.

items.sentence: Seeing Patricia cry like that made me feel guilty. -> What ___
items.answer: What made me feel guilty was seeing Patricia cry like that.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence using 'What'.

items.sentence: What we had to do first was decide where to meet. -> The first thing ___
items.answer: The first thing we had to do was decide where to meet.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence starting with 'The first thing'.

items.sentence: There are lots of facilities where we go camping. -> The place ___
items.answer: The place where we go camping has lots of facilities.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence focusing on the place.

items.sentence: Einstein proved that energy and mass are basically the same thing. -> What ___
items.answer: What Einstein proved was that energy and mass are basically the same thing.
items.explanation: Cleft sentence using 'What'.

exercises.label: G
exercises.instruction: Write so, such, too or enough in each gap.

items.sentence: It was ___ good a meal that we left the waiter quite a large tip.
items.explanation: 'so + adjective + a + noun' is a correct structure.

items.sentence: It's becoming increasingly clear that there just aren't ___ people willing to buy our products online.
items.explanation: 'enough' comes before a noun to mean 'sufficient'.

items.sentence: I'm quite enjoying my maths degree, but I just find statistics ___ boring!
items.explanation: 'so' is used with an adjective to mean 'very'.

items.sentence: Angela seemed to be having ___ a good time at the party that I decided not to tell her how late it was.
items.explanation: 'such a + adjective + noun' is the standard structure.

items.sentence: We've all been working ___ hard on the latest project the manager gave us a couple of extra days off.
items.explanation: 'so + adverb' followed by a result clause (with 'that' omitted).

items.sentence: I worry about my grandparents because they have ___ a lot of health problems.
items.explanation: 'such a lot of' is a common expression.

items.sentence: It's ___ early to tell whether the business is going to succeed or not.
items.explanation: 'too' here means 'more than is desirable' to make a judgment.

items.sentence: Set the alarm or we'll never wake up early ___ to catch the first train.
items.explanation: 'enough' comes after an adverb.

items.sentence: I never knew that Andrew had ___ few friends.
items.explanation: 'so few' is used with plural nouns.

items.sentence: Everyone was surprised when Dan failed because he's ___ a good student.
items.explanation: 'such a + adjective + noun' for emphasis.

items.sentence: A year after losing my job, I had ___ many debts that I was really beginning to panic.
items.explanation: 'so many' is used with plural nouns.

items.sentence: I was hoping to have a word with Alan before he left, but it seems to be ___ late now.
items.explanation: 'too late' means it is no longer possible.

exercises.label: H
exercises.instruction: Complete the second sentence so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence, using the word given. Do not change the word given. You must use between three and eight words, including the word given.

items.sentence: I had to clear the spare room before I could start decorating. -> Only when ___ start decorating. (cleared)
items.answer: I had cleared the spare room could I
items.explanation: 'Only when' clause is followed by an inverted main clause.

items.sentence: The phone rang again as soon as I put it down. -> Hardly ___ it rang again. (had)
items.answer: had I put the phone down when
items.explanation: 'Hardly + had + S + V3 + when' is the required structure.

items.sentence: The Watsons moved to London and very soon after they decided to get divorced. -> No sooner ___ they decided to get divorced. (had)
items.answer: had the Watsons moved to London than
items.explanation: 'No sooner + had + S + V3 + than' is the required structure.

items.sentence: I had no idea the manager wanted to see me to fire me. -> Little ___ the manager wanted to see me to fire me. (realise)
items.explanation: 'Little did I realise' is the standard inverted phrase for lack of knowledge.

items.sentence: I was told about the website by Charlie. -> It ___ me about the website. (who)
items.explanation: Cleft sentence: It + was + [person] + who + verb.

items.sentence: Buying a plane ticket at the last minute isn't often possible. -> Rarely ___ a plane ticket at the last minute. (to)
items.explanation: 'Rarely is it possible...' uses inversion after the negative adverb.

items.sentence: They didn't ask me anything about my plans for the summer. -> Not ___ about my plans for the summer. (question)
items.answer: a (single) question did they ask me
items.explanation: 'Not a single question did they ask' uses inversion for emphasis.

items.sentence: There is no situation in which we will allow the management to lower wages. -> Under ___ the management to lower wages. (no)
items.explanation: 'Under no circumstances' triggers inversion.

items.sentence: Not until we have no choice ___ the business down.
items.explanation: 'Not until...' requires inversion in the main clause.

items.sentence: It was ___ a victory that even Smith's fans couldn't believe it.
items.explanation: 'so + adjective + a + noun' is valid, but here it is just 'so + adjective' followed by 'that'.

items.sentence: At no time ___ I was going to fail the exam.
items.explanation: 'At no time' requires inversion.

items.sentence: So ___ that they actually finished three weeks early.
items.explanation: 'So + adjective + verb + subject' is the emphatic inversion structure.

items.sentence: Never ___ complete and utter rubbish!
items.explanation: 'Never have I heard such + noun' is the correct inversion and usage of 'such'.

items.sentence: In no way ___ that people will be prevented from organising peaceful protests.
items.explanation: 'In no way' requires inversion.

items.sentence: If you don't put the tent up ___ , it might blow away!
items.explanation: 'adjective/adverb + enough' means to the necessary degree.

items.sentence: No sooner ___ everyone started to gossip about him.
items.explanation: 'No sooner... than' with past perfect inversion.

items.sentence: 'I can't stand this hot weather!' 'No, ___ . Dreadful, isn't it?'
items.explanation: 'Neither/Nor' is used to agree with a negative statement.

items.sentence: 'We were at the Rolling Stones concert last weekend.' 'Were you? So ___ .'
items.explanation: 'So' is used to agree with a positive statement, followed by inversion.

exercises.label: J
exercises.instruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: Rarely ___ neighbours have the best of relationships for any length of time.
items.explanation: Inversion after 'Rarely' in the present simple.

items.sentence: ...but only if there is a lot of give and take ___ this possible.
items.explanation: Inversion in the main clause after 'only if...'.

items.sentence: ___ common are disputes between neighbours that there are many laws...
items.explanation: Part of the 'So + adjective... that' structure for emphasis.

items.sentence: Before things go ___ far for the relationship to be saved...
items.explanation: 'too far to be saved' (more than is acceptable).

items.sentence: The first is ___ speak to your neighbour.
items.explanation: 'The first (thing) is to [verb]'.

items.sentence: You'd be amazed at how many arguments are avoided by ___ a simple step.
items.explanation: 'such a + adjective + noun'.

items.sentence: Not ___ does it give you the chance to express your complaint...
items.explanation: Part of 'Not only... (but) also'.

items.sentence: ...it ___ gives your neighbour the chance to explain...
items.explanation: Completing 'Not only... but also'.

items.sentence: On ___ account should you do anything to damage your neighbours' property...
items.explanation: 'On no account' triggers inversion and means 'never'.

items.sentence: ___ until you are sure you are acting within the law should you do anything.
items.explanation: 'Not until...' triggers inversion in the main clause ('should you do anything').
